title: Foreword
description: Purpose and scope of the guide
content:
UK wastewater projects succeed when design quality, safe buildability,
and right-first-time commissioning align. This booklet is a practical
companion for everyone on a new-build WWTW---from designers and MEICA
engineers to site managers, commissioners and QS teams. It is not a
replacement for contract requirements or permits; instead it distils
what good looks like and where projects most often slip.

Defects cost more the later they are found. A simple planning lapse can
cost 1 to fix at the desktop, 10 in construction, and 100 in
commissioning or operations. In our sector the multiplier is often
worse: rework can jeopardise consents, prolong outages, and trigger
liquidated damages or environmental harm. Typical root causes are
familiar---unclear scope, missing interfaces, late design changes,
unverified instrumentation ranges, rushed FAT/SAT, and inadequate ITPs
or hold points. The remedy is equally familiar: clear responsibilities,
early checks, and disciplined handovers.

Use the stage tabs to jump straight to Pre-Treatment, Primary, Secondary
(ASP, MBR, IFAS, MBBR, Nereda, MOB), Post-Treatment, Sludge, and the
cross-cutting systems that touch every stage. Each system has a short
How it works panel and six crisp lists: Key
design/construction/commissioning considerations and their common
errors. Checklists and Hold Points conclude each stage so teams can
agree what "done" means before moving on.

Throughout, align decisions and records with CESWI 8, WIMES, MCERTS, CDM
2015, and your EA Permit. Apply them proportionately, but never
casually. If in doubt, escalate early---especially where changes affect
hydraulics, safety, process guarantees, or sampling/monitoring
integrity.

Thank you for contributing to safer sites, compliant assets, and
resilient treatment for the communities we serve. Let's build once,
commission cleanly, and hand over with confidence.

title: Standards Snapshot
description: CESWI 8, WIMES, MCERTS, CDM 2015 and EA Permits

directSubsections.title: Purpose
directSubsections.content:
This page summarises the core UK frameworks that govern new-build
wastewater treatment works. Use it to check which standard applies, what
evidence is required, and who is responsible before design release,
construction, or commissioning.

directSubsections.title: CESWI 8 --- Civil Engineering Specification for the Water Industry
directSubsections.content:
-   Scope: civil/structural works, materials, workmanship, testing,
    durability, finishes.

-   Use: forms the baseline for drawings, specs, method statements, and
    ITPs.

-   Triggers: concrete mix design and curing, cover to reinforcement,
    waterstops/joints, backfill/compaction, coatings/linings,
    watertightness tests.

-   Evidence: ITPs with hold points (formwork/rebar inspections, pour
    releases, pressure/watertightness tests), concrete cube results,
    coating DFT/holiday tests.

directSubsections.title: WIMES --- Water Industry Mechanical and Electrical Specifications
directSubsections.content:
-   Scope: MEICA equipment (pumps, blowers, MCCs, instruments, chemical
    systems).

-   Use: dictates duty/standby, materials, ingress protection, paint
    systems, noise/vibration, maintainability, spares, documentation.

-   Triggers: vendor selection, datasheet sign-off, FAT/SAT scope, O&M
    content, asset tagging.

-   Evidence: compliance matrix, certified datasheets, FAT/SAT plans and
    results, wiring/loop checks, earthing tests, nameplate/asset data.

directSubsections.title: MCERTS --- Monitoring Certification (Environment Agency)
directSubsections.content:
-   Scope: flow measurement to/at consents and final effluent monitoring
    (where applicable).

-   Use: ensures devices, installation, calibration and data meet
    regulatory performance.

-   Triggers: inlet/outfall flumes, ultrasonic/DP meters, samplers,
    final effluent monitors, data acquisition.

-   Evidence: design review against MCERTS guidance, installation
    survey, calibration certificate, verification report, maintenance
    plan, data integrity checks.

directSubsections.title: CDM 2015 --- Construction (Design and Management) Regulations
directSubsections.content:
-   Scope: roles, responsibilities and coordination for health and
    safety through design, build and handover.

-   Use: informs risk elimination/reduction, methods,
    access/maintenance, and information flow.

-   Triggers: appointment of duty holders, pre-construction information,
    design risk management, construction phase plan.

-   Evidence: design risk register, residual risk notes on drawings,
    CPP, RAMS, competency records, close-out handover of H&S file.

directSubsections.title: EA Environmental Permit (and variations)
directSubsections.content:
-   Scope: consent limits, monitoring requirements, operating
    techniques, reporting.

-   Use: drives process selection, hydraulic capacity, bypass/storm
    arrangements, instrumentation ranges and sampling points.

-   Triggers: any change affecting consent compliance, sampling, or
    bypass behaviour.

-   Evidence: permit and variations, dispersion/hydraulic assessments,
    monitoring positions, alarm/reporting configuration, compliance
    demonstration.

directSubsections.title: Contract precedence and conflicts
directSubsections.content:
-   Employer's Requirements / Works Information → Contract →
    Statute/Permit → National Standards (CESWI 8, WIMES) →
    Manufacturer's instructions.

-   Resolve gaps/conflicts via early technical query/RFI and documented
    change control.

-   Where a manufacturer's requirement exceeds the baseline spec, adopt
    the higher standard unless the client approves otherwise.

directSubsections.title: Responsibility at a glance (typical split)
directSubsections.content:
-   Client/Employer: sets outcomes, accepts risks/variations, owns
    permit.

-   Principal Designer: coordinates pre-construction information,
    manages design risk.

-   Designers (Civils/MEICA/ICA): deliver compliant designs, identify
    residual risks, define ITP hold points.

-   Principal Contractor: plans and controls construction, implements
    ITPs/RAMS, maintains records.

-   MEICA Vendors/OEMs: prove WIMES compliance, execute FAT/SAT, supply
    O&M/asset data.

-   Commissioning Lead: integrates plans, proves performance, protects
    consent during transition.

-   Quality Manager: assures evidence, manages non-conformances and
    close-out.

directSubsections.title: Gate deliverables to check before proceeding
directSubsections.content:
-   Design: basis and calculations, drawings/specs, WIMES/MCERTS
    compliance matrix, design risk register, ITP/hold points defined.

-   Construction: RAMS and temporary works, materials approvals,
    inspection/test results, calibration certificates, updated redlines.

-   Commissioning: FAT/SAT results, commissioning plan, temporary
    operating envelope, evidence against permit limits, handover pack
    (O&M, asset data, training).

title: The Cost of Defects
description: Why defects cost more the later they are found

directSubsections.title: Purpose
directSubsections.content:
Defects become more expensive the later they are discovered. On
new-build WWTW, the impact is not only direct rework but also programme
slippage, extended outages, vendor re-visits, and potential permit
non-compliance.

directSubsections.title: Why costs escalate
directSubsections.content:
Early issues are cheap to change on paper. Once civils, MEICA, software,
and operations are coupled, a small error can drive demolition, re-pour,
re-rigging, re-cabling, re-calibration, re-testing, and
re-witnessing---often under time pressure to protect consent.

directSubsections.title: 1--10--100 framing
directSubsections.content:
-   1: Fix at design desk (spec/drawing/datasheet) through review or TQ.

-   10: Fix during construction (materials, labour, temporary works,
    retest).

-   100: Fix in commissioning/operation (outages, vendor
    re-mobilisation, consent risk, potential LADs).

directSubsections.title: Illustrative UK order-of-magnitude bands
directSubsections.content:
-   Design correction (pre-IFC): £500--£5k (calculations, redrawing,
    coordination).

-   Minor civils rework: £10k--£50k (break-out, re-form/re-pour,
    waterproofing, tests).

-   MEICA installation rework: £15k--£80k (re-mount, re-pipe, re-cable,
    terminations, inspections).

-   Instrumentation/MCERTS non-compliance: £10k--£60k (re-site, new
    primary device, verification).

-   Process functional changes (software/controls): £5k--£40k (code
    changes, FAT/SAT re-runs, alarms).

-   Commissioning delay with vendor returns: £25k--£150k
    (re-mobilisation, hire, temporary treatment).

-   Consent breach incident: scale-dependent; can exceed £100k+
    including mitigation, sampling, reporting, potential penalties, and
    reputational cost.

-   Programme/LAD exposure: per contract---model exposure where critical
    path is affected.

directSubsections.title: Common late-stage cost drivers
directSubsections.content:
-   Hydraulic mis-levels/crest elevations; inadequate headroom for
    losses.

-   Wrong duty/standby philosophy or access envelopes.

-   Incompatible materials in aggressive environments; coating/lining
    failures.

-   Mis-sized blowers/pumps due to incorrect design bases or
    ranges/units.

-   PLC/SCADA ranges, scaling, and alarming mismatched to instruments.

-   MCERTS primary device geometry/installation outside tolerances.

-   Missing hold points; uncontrolled changes; incomplete redlines/O&M
    data.

directSubsections.title: Controls that reduce total cost
directSubsections.content:
-   Define and freeze design bases (flows, loads, temperatures,
    consent).

-   Plan ITPs with hold points that visibly stop work before
    irreversible steps (reinforcement inspection, pour release,
    pressure/watertightness tests, cable tests, software lock-down).

-   Specify WIMES compliance in procurement with traceable evidence.

-   Apply CESWI 8 workmanship checks for durability and watertightness.

-   Build MCERTS into design and installation (primary device selection,
    location, straight lengths, access for verification).

-   Run proportionate FAT/SAT with clear acceptance criteria and witness
    plans.

-   Tight change control: TQs early, update drawings, update ITPs,
    record residual risks.

-   Measure and report cost-of-quality (prevention, appraisal, internal
    failure, external failure) monthly.

**Note**

Values above are indicative to frame decisions, not a substitute for
contract pricing or risk analysis. Always confirm contractual LADs,
client approval routes, and permit implications before changes.

subsystems.title: Whole-Works Flow (Influent to Final Effluent)
subsystems.content:
A modern municipal wastewater treatment works receives incoming flows
and routes them through a sequence of clearly defined stages that
progressively remove screenings, grit and fats, settle and thicken
solids, and biologically oxidise organic matter and nutrients to meet
consent. The process then polishes remaining fine solids and disinfects
the effluent before a compliant, well evidenced discharge. Exact
configurations vary by permit limits, flows and loads, available
footprint, geotechnical constraints, and whole life considerations, but
the interfaces between stages must remain clear and verifiable. Designs
should preserve hydraulic capacity, mixing and sampling integrity, while
providing reliable isolation, bypass and access for maintenance. During
construction and commissioning, evidence based checks confirm levels,
headloss budgets, instrumentation ranges and control narratives, proving
that the integrated system performs as intended. The outcome is
resilient, operable treatment that protects the environment, withstands
storms and outages, and hands over with defensible records that satisfy
regulators and operators alike.

subsections.title: MAIN STAGES AND TYPICAL SYSTEMS
subsections.content:
-   Pre-Treatment: Coarse and fine screens with enclosed screenings
    handling; aerated or vortex grit removal; dedicated FOG capture
    systems; inlet pumps and wet wells sized for storms; overflow weirs
    and storm tanks with controlled returns; odour covers, washdowns and
    safe access that protect downstream assets reliably during severe
    weather.

-   Primary Treatment: Primary settlement tanks or lamella clarifiers
    with scum removal, sludge withdrawal pumps and launder covers,
    producing stable primary sludge and clarified effluent for
    downstream biological treatment.

-   Secondary (Biological) Treatment: Activated sludge, MBR, IFAS, MBBR,
    Nereda or MOB processes with diffused aeration; selectors and
    internal recycles; RAS and SAS pumping; clarifiers where required;
    mixing, instrumentation and control to meet ammonia, BOD and
    phosphorus limits reliably across seasons and temperature.

-   Post-Treatment (Tertiary and Disinfection): Chemical phosphorus
    removal with coagulant and alkali trim; tertiary solids filtration
    using cloth, disc or sand units; DAF polishing where justified; UV
    disinfection sized on UVT and flow; monitored final discharge
    structures with representative sampling and MCERTS certified flow
    measurement.

-   Cross-Stage Systems (sitewide): MCERTS-compliant flow measurement
    and quality monitoring; odour capture and treatment; electrical
    distribution with standby generation and UPS; ICA, SCADA and
    telemetry with cybersecurity; chemical storage, bunding and dosing
    systems; dirty and clean drainage segregation; access, lifting and
    maintainability provisions; quality assurance, ITPs and handover;
    programme, cost and carbon management; CDM roles, design risk,
    emergency response and a usable health and safety file system.

subsections.title: KEY INTERFACES TO PLAN AND VERIFY
subsections.content:
-   Hydraulic levels, freeboards and headloss allowances between units,
    verified across clean and fouled conditions.

-   Valve, penstock and bypass arrangements for isolation and
    resilience, with tested seals, access, interlocks and clearly
    labelled positions.

-   Sampling and monitoring locations, ranges and straight-length needs,
    aligned to MCERTS and analyser requirements for reliability.

-   Access, lifting, washdown and drainage designed for safe operations
    and maintenance, with platforms, lighting, fall protection and
    trapped gullies everywhere.

-   Data, alarms and handover criteria aligned with permits and
    standards, including time synchronisation, historian tags and
    acceptance evidence requirements clearly.

subsystems.title: Sludge and Energy Loop (Solids Line and Liquors Return)
subsystems.content:
Primary and secondary sludge are thickened and stabilised to minimise
mass, recover energy and produce a transportable final product that
meets outlet and logistics requirements reliably all year. Liquors from
thickening, digestion heating circuits and dewatering return to the head
of works under controlled rates, or receive dedicated side-stream
treatment when loads justify investment and footprint. Correct
integration of the sludge line is critical to whole-works performance,
carbon footprint, permit resilience and operating cost; decisions on
polymers, digestion, heat balance, gas use, liquor timing and cake
logistics directly influence upstream biology, odour risk, winter
operability and evidence for compliance. The best outcomes come from
disciplined mass balances, robust hydraulics and proven commissioning
playbooks.

subsections.title: SLUDGE LINE STAGES AND TYPICAL SYSTEMS
subsections.content:
-   Thickening: DAF, drum or gravity belt thickeners with duty and
    standby trains, supported by reliable polymer make-up, ageing,
    dilution control and safe dosing points that protect capture,
    filtrate quality and downstream digestion stability during realistic
    seasonal operating envelopes.

-   Stabilisation and energy: Mesophilic or thermophilic anaerobic
    digestion with dependable heating, mixing and foam control,
    integrated gas handling, H2S treatment, CHP or boilers for heat
    recovery, and enclosed emergency flares sized for trips, outages and
    cold-start conditions credibly.

-   Dewatering: Decanter centrifuges or plate-screw presses delivering
    dry, stackable cake to enclosed conveyors, intermediate storage and
    weather-resilient loading bays, with odour capture, leachate
    drainage and interlocks that prevent back-up into upstream machines
    during routine operations.

-   Liquors management: Equalisation tanks with mixing, monitored
    returns paced to influent flow or aeration headroom, and side-stream
    treatment such as deammonification where ammonia load or temperature
    would otherwise destabilise the main works during ordinary duty and
    storm recovery.

subsections.title: INTEGRATION POINTS THAT AFFECT COMPLIANCE AND COST
subsections.content:
-   Liquors flow equalisation to avoid shock loading the secondary
    process, using paced returns, conservative setpoints and seasonal
    envelopes that protect nitrification and clarifier stability
    consistently.

-   Odour containment at tanks, hatches, conveyors and cake bays, with
    negative pressure capture, treatment and trapped drainage that
    preserve neighbour amenity and minimise corrosion exposures onsite.

-   Corrosion protection and materials compatibility in aggressive
    environments, combining specified coatings, FRP or 316L selections,
    isolation kits and disciplined detailing in persistent vapour zones.

-   Energy recovery balance (heat and electricity) versus resilience and
    maintenance, aligning CHP duty, boiler backup, flare capacity and
    outage modes to keep digesters within temperature limits reliably.

-   Road access, weighbridge interfaces and containment for cake
    logistics, including turning, marshalling, telescopic chutes, spill
    control, wheel wash and documented duty-of-care evidence end-to-end.

title: Pre-Treatment
description: Screening, grit removal, inlet pumping and storm management
content:
Pre-treatment protects the entire works by removing coarse and fine
debris and grit before they can damage pumps, foul membranes, block
pipework, or upset biological processes. It also manages hydraulic
surges and first-flush loads, provides secure inlet pumping via the pump
station to maintain downstream head, and routes exceptional flows to
storm storage or overflow arrangements as permitted. Success at this
stage is measured by reliable debris capture and handling, controlled
headloss, safe operability, and clean interfaces to primary treatment.

subsections.title: How It Works
subsections.content:
Fine screens remove smaller solids that pass coarse bars, protecting
pumps, aeration equipment and downstream processes (especially membrane
systems). Common types are perforated-plate band/escalator, stepped and
continuous belt screens, typically with apertures around 3--6 mm
(tighter for membrane pretreatment as required by the vendor). Flow
approaches the screen face in a channel; debris is retained while water
passes through. As blinding increases, differential level rises and the
screen initiates a cleaning cycle using rakes or travelling panels plus
spray bars. Captured screenings discharge to a washpress or
washer--compactor, reducing organics, free water and odour before sealed
skip export. Designs must provide a duty/standby philosophy or multiple
lanes, a positively isolatable bypass, appropriate headloss budget,
robust materials and coatings for corrosive atmospheres, and safe access
and lifting. Washwater, drainage, ventilation/odour control and
electrical segregation must be integrated. Acceptance depends on proven
capture, controlled headloss across flows, reliable cleaning cycles, and
screenings handling sized for peak rag loads. Reference CESWI 8 for
civils/durability and WIMES for MEICA compliance.

subsections.title: KEY DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS
subsections.content:
-   Confirm design flows, storm peaking factors and allowable headloss
    envelopes across clean and blinded conditions.

-   Select screen type and aperture using downstream sensitivity, debris
    character, temperature and acceptable washwater consumption.

-   Provide duty and standby lanes with isolation gates, plus a true
    bypass fitted with emergency screening.

-   Size washer compactor, transfer chutes and drainage to match
    screenings load, ensuring sealed connections and containment.

-   Integrate washwater supply with verified pressure, strainers and
    backflow prevention suitable for foul duty and cleaning.

-   Allow safe access, lighting, platforms and certified lifting points
    so routine maintenance never requires temporary works.

-   Specify corrosion resistant materials, fixings and coatings proven
    for hydrogen sulphide, saline aerosols and frequent washdowns.

-   Position differential level sensors with adequate straight lengths,
    away from turbulence, providing safe calibration access.

-   Provide freeze protection for spray bars, valves and pipework;
    specify insulation, trace heating and drain-down provisions.

subsections.title: COMMON DESIGN ERRORS
subsections.content:
-   Aperture selected too coarse for membranes or tertiary filters,
    allowing damaging fibres and fines through consistently.

-   Blinding headloss underestimated at winter viscosity, causing
    upstream flooding risk and frequent nuisance alarms during storms.

-   No positive isolation or emergency screening on bypass, forcing
    unsafe interventions and exporting debris downstream repeatedly.

-   Screenings handling undersized for rag loads, causing blockages,
    odour issues, spills and extended operator interventions.

-   Poor drainage from washer compactor spreads leachate, contaminates
    clean networks and creates persistent hygiene problems.

-   Differential sensors sited near bends or turbulence give unreliable
    control, nuisance cleaning cycles and misleading headloss trends.

-   Spray system freeze risk overlooked, causing burst lines,
    uncontrolled leaks and lengthy winter outages awaiting replacement.

subsections.title: KEY CONSTRUCTION CONSIDERATIONS
subsections.content:
-   Set frames plumb, level and square, verifying panel flatness and
    clearances before introducing any flows.

-   Seal frame perimeters and wall interfaces carefully to prevent side
    bypass and uncontrolled leakage under load.

-   Install spray bars to vendor spacing with correct nozzles, strainers
    and valves; pressure test before operation.

-   Provide continuous falls to screenings drainage with trapped
    connections, avoiding pooling, aerosols and odour release.

-   Align discharge chutes precisely to sealed skips or conveyors,
    minimising spillage, windblown litter and manual cleaning.

-   Route cables clear of wash zones and maintenance areas, with drip
    loops, correct glands and labelling.

-   Prove anchorages and fixed lifting points with certificates and test
    loads; document safe working loads clearly.

-   Install and function-test guards and interlocks before energisation,
    ensuring moving parts are inaccessible during commissioning
    activities.

-   Balance spray bars and flush strainers thoroughly before wet runs to
    prevent streaking, re-fouling and uneven blinding.

subsections.title: COMMON CONSTRUCTION ERRORS
subsections.content:
-   Twisted or racked frame causes panels to bind, elevating friction,
    trips and early component wear.

-   Gaps at walls or poor seals create bypass paths, leaking solids and
    undermining measured capture performance.

-   Leaking or misaligned spray bars give streaky cleaning, progressive
    blinding and rising headloss during service.

-   Drain lines without fall or blocked traps accumulate solids,
    generate odour and hinder routine housekeeping.

-   Cabling routed across access paths obstructs maintenance, suffers
    damage and creates foreseeable safety hazards.

-   Missing or defeated interlocks and guards expose operators to
    entanglement hazards during early tests and maintenance access.

subsections.title: KEY COMMISSIONING CONSIDERATIONS
subsections.content:
-   Dry run drives, verify torque protection and overload trips,
    direction, limits and emergency stop functionality.

-   Wet test headloss across full duty range and staged blinding,
    confirming upstream levels and alarms.

-   Set differential level start and stop points; tune cleaning triggers
    to minimise water use effectively.

-   Verify washer compactor dewatering performance, dryness targets and
    sealed drainage routing under representative loading.

-   Prove duty, assist and standby rotation, jam detection responses and
    alarm paths across realistic debris scenarios.

-   Test bypass operation under flow, including isolation gates, seals,
    emergency screening and clear operator procedures.

-   Record baseline capture, blinding rates and cycle counts to enable
    early detection of deterioration.

-   Perform loss-of-utilities drills---washwater, instrument air and
    power---verifying safe states, alarms, manual overrides and restart
    behaviours.

-   Establish seasonal setpoints for cleaning triggers and wash
    durations, optimising energy use and avoiding winter headloss
    excursions.

subsections.title: COMMON COMMISSIONING ERRORS
subsections.content:
-   Trip thresholds set too low cause nuisance stops; too high risks
    damage during jams.

-   Spray pressure or flow insufficient for cleaning leaves fouling,
    rising headloss and unreliable capture.

-   Washer compactor overloaded by mis-sized screen or high debris,
    causing spills, odour and downtime.

-   Level sensors uncalibrated or poorly located produce misleading
    trends and delayed interventions during storms.

-   No spares, nozzle kits or maintenance plan leads to prolonged
    outages and inconsistent shift responses.

-   Bypass not exercised under flow; first live event reveals leaks,
    contamination risks and poorly rehearsed operator actions.

-   Inadequate loss-of-utilities testing leaves teams unprepared for
    washwater interruptions, causing uncontrolled spillage and downtime.

subsections.title: How it works
subsections.content:
Screenings handling systems receive solids lifted from coarse and fine
screens and condition them for safe, compliant disposal. Typically,
screenings drop into a washer--compactor via a chute or enclosed
conveyor. The wash stage removes entrained organics with controlled
spray pressure and flow; the compaction stage squeezes free water and
reduces volume before discharge to a sealed bin or skip. Some plants use
screw presses or twin-shaft compactors; others incorporate bagging units
for transport or odour control. Drainage liquor returns to the inlet
channel or a dedicated sump with appropriate traps and backflow
protection. Designs must manage rag mass variability, first-flush events
and temperature effects on grease. Reliable operation depends on
adequate washwater supply, correct chute angles and transitions, odour
containment, drainage falls, compatible materials, safe access and
lifting, and clear interfaces for bin change-out and traffic management.
Performance is judged by dryness, mass reduction, odour control, capture
integrity, low downtime and clean, ergonomic operation consistent with
CESWI 8 and WIMES expectations.

subsections.title: Key design considerations
subsections.content:
-   Determine design, peak and first-flush flows and allowable headloss
    across the complete screenings line for robust hydraulic
    performance.

-   Right-size washer--compactor duty, motor torque and throughput to
    worst-case rag loads, moisture content and bin change-out frequency
    targets.

-   Provide enclosed chutes and conveyors with self-clearing geometry,
    effective drainage, odour containment, and accessible clean-out
    ports for safe maintenance.

-   Integrate a hygienic washwater source with verified backflow
    protection, isolation valves, strainers and temperature limits
    suitable for grease mobilisation.

-   Plan sealed skip size, site access, traffic routes, turning circles
    and lifting clearances to avoid unsafe manoeuvres and delays during
    collections.

-   Specify materials, protective coatings and fasteners compatible with
    corrosive, abrasive, and biologically aggressive screenings
    environments over design life.

-   Incorporate safe access platforms, lighting, guarding and ergonomics
    that enable routine tasks without manual handling or unsafe body
    positioning.

-   Interlock washer--compactor to bin presence and fullness, preventing
    discharge without container, with overfill alarms and lockouts.

-   Provide sealed transfer chutes and drainage fall to trapped sumps,
    with washwater backflow protection and hygienic clean-down access.

subsections.title: Common design errors
subsections.content:
-   Underestimating rag mass and variability leads to blockages,
    compactor trips, frequent manual intervention and unacceptable
    operational downtime during storms.

-   Insufficient freeboard, shallow chute angles or poor transitions
    cause bridging, spillage and chronic housekeeping burdens at peak
    hydraulic conditions.

-   Inadequate drainage falls and sump design leave stagnant liquor,
    creating odours, corrosion risk and unacceptable operator exposure
    to contaminated liquids.

-   Lack of resilience for washwater supply interrupts dewatering
    performance, increases skip weights significantly and drives
    unnecessary transport cost impacts.

-   Upstream aperture selection too coarse reduces capture efficiency,
    overloading fine screens, washpress capacity and downstream
    screenings logistics unexpectedly.

-   Undersized washer--compactor relative to peak rag mass causes
    spillage, conveyors choking, odour complaints and unplanned
    maintenance.

-   Skip change-out envelope omitted, restricting vehicle access,
    forcing manual handling, increasing accident risk and turnaround
    time.

subsections.title: Key construction considerations
subsections.content:
-   Install frames, chutes and conveyors plumb and true so transfers
    align precisely, avoiding pinch points, hang-ups and damaging
    contact during operation cycles.

-   Seal wall and slab penetrations meticulously to prevent bypass
    leakage paths, unsanitary conditions and concealed corrosion that
    undermines durability expectations.

-   Construct drainage with continuous fall to trapped collection
    points, eliminating dead legs and reducing the likelihood of
    recurrent blockages and maintenance interventions.

-   Prove backflow prevention devices, isolation valves and hose
    stations are accessible, correctly oriented, tagged and permanently
    identifiable for routine inspections.

-   Route cabling clear of moving parts; provide adequate slack,
    mechanical protection and segregation to reduce abrasion, snagging
    and electrical reliability issues.

-   Commission lifting beams, anchor points and platforms with
    certificates before introducing loads, ensuring safe bin exchanges
    and component replacement activities.

-   Install bin presence sensors, emergency stops and guarding before
    energisation; function-test interlocks with empty and full
    containers.

-   Set and verify spray flows, strainers and nozzles; balance washwater
    to achieve target dryness without excessive carryover.

subsections.title: Common construction errors
subsections.content:
-   Misaligned chute transitions create persistent screenings hang-ups,
    forcing manual poking and repeated unsafe operator interventions
    during normal operating conditions.

-   Washwater pipework insufficiently flushed leaves debris lodging in
    spray bars, causing uneven cleaning performance and rapid re-fouling
    shortly after start-up.

-   Poorly sealed joints and gland plates permit liquid escape,
    producing slip hazards, corrosion staining and premature coating
    failures around equipment bases.

-   Unprotected or loosely supported cables cross access routes,
    becoming damaged by bins, skips and routine maintenance traffic
    during daily screening operations.

-   Missing guards or non-functional interlocks expose operators to
    conveyors, augers or reciprocating mechanisms while cleaning tasks
    are undertaken at pace.

-   Drain branches lack isolation valves, spreading contamination during
    maintenance, causing hygiene issues and prolonged plant downtime.

-   Guards removed for access and not reinstated, leaving nip points
    exposed around augers, conveyors and compactor drive shafts.

subsections.title: Key commissioning considerations
subsections.content:
-   Demonstrate automatic cycles under representative rag loads, logging
    torque, current draw and cycle time distributions to establish
    robust operational baselines.

-   Establish baseline dryness, mass reduction and capture efficiency
    against defined test loads and flows, documenting repeatable
    acceptance evidence for handover.

-   Optimise wash durations, spray pressures and compaction backpressure
    to balance dryness, throughput, noise and wear without compromising
    downstream logistics.

-   Prove failure modes and recoveries: blocked chute, jammed auger,
    full skip alarm, washwater loss and emergency stop, including safe
    restart procedures.

-   Verify alarms, interlocks and permissives integrate with SCADA
    sequences, local controls and defined manual override procedures for
    maintenance scenarios correctly.

-   Train operators in safe clearing methods, lockout steps, hygiene
    protocols, skip change procedures and emergency stop recovery
    processes before live operation.

-   Baseline screenings dryness and mass-reduction curves; record motor
    currents, cycle times and alarms at representative flow conditions.

-   Prove bin change-out sequence under load, including auto-stop on
    removal, restart interlocks and spill containment arrangements.

subsections.title: Common commissioning errors
subsections.content:
-   Accepting performance at light rag loads only hides overload
    behaviours that emerge during storms, leading to unexpected trips
    and loss of screening capacity.

-   Failing to calibrate torque or current thresholds results in
    nuisance trips or undetected jams that damage flights, screws or
    drive components during operation.

-   Neglecting compaction backpressure tuning yields wet, heavy
    screenings that increase disposal costs and create odour issues
    during handling and transport.

-   Skipping loss-of-utilities testing leaves teams unprepared for
    washwater or power interruptions, causing uncontrolled spillage and
    prolonged downtime events.

-   Incomplete O&M data and spares lists hinder preventive maintenance
    planning, reducing reliability and increasing lifecycle costs across
    the screening facility.

-   Accepting dryness without calibrated scales and timestamps
    undermines evidence, hiding capacity shortfalls and increasing
    disposal costs.

-   Failure to test emergency stops and interlocks under live conditions
    leads to unsafe overrides and undetected logic gaps.

subsystems.title: Grit Removal (Free Vortex)
subsystems.content:
Grit removal protects pumps, valves and downstream process units by
extracting dense inorganic particles---sand, silt and mineral
fines---before they abrade equipment or accumulate in tanks. **A single
arrangement is used:** free vortex into a circular detritor with grit
rakes. Bottom scrapers or rakes sweep settled grit to a central or
peripheral sump; a grit pump transfers the slurry to washing and
classification. Scum boards and peripheral launders protect outlets,
while adjustable rake torque and wear liners manage abrasive duty.
Effective designs set channel velocities, swirl strength and detention
times to achieve the target cut size across average and storm flows,
while limiting headloss and avoiding short-circuiting. Success depends
on a robust hydraulic profile, abrasion-resistant materials, reliable
purge and pumping sequences, odour and aerosol management, safe access
and lifting, and maintainable instrumentation suited to sandy, highly
abrasive environments.

subsections.title: KEY DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS
subsections.content:
-   Define design, peak and first-flush flows; size detention time and
    swirl so the target particle cut size is achieved without
    impractical channel footprints or excessive headloss.

-   Free vortex detritor: size tank diameter, side slopes and rake duty;
    check torque margins, wear liners and extraction sump geometry so
    rakes remain self-clearing under worst loads.

-   Shape all hoppers with self-clearing slopes and smooth transitions,
    placing extraction points to prevent rat-holing, dead zones and
    hardening between purge cycles.

-   Specify abrasion-resistant materials, coatings and replaceable wear
    parts for pumps, bends and valves, including hardened elbows, liners
    and sacrificial pieces on high-velocity routes.

-   Integrate a correctly sized grit washer--classifier with overflow
    returns, trapped drainage, odour control and safe access, setting
    dryness and organics limits to disposal acceptance.

-   Provide duty/standby extraction pumps and resilient power/control
    philosophies so purge, transfer and washing continue safely during
    outages, trips or maintenance interventions.

-   Define target cut size, allowable organics content and acceptance
    test methods with sampling points, weighing procedures and
    repeatability expectations agreed in the specification.

subsections.title: COMMON DESIGN ERRORS
subsections.content:
-   Underestimating grit load or particle size distribution causes
    accumulation, hopper choking, manual digging and progressive
    abrasion of pumps, valves and mixers downstream.

-   Inlet geometry and swirl development poorly defined;
    short-circuiting occurs, separation suffers and putrescible organics
    remain in the grit, increasing odour and disposal cost.

-   Hopper or base slopes too shallow; self-clearing fails, creating
    hardened deposits that later demand intrusive and hazardous
    confined-space cleaning interventions.

-   Suction lines overlong or elbowed sharply; extraction efficiency
    collapses, wear escalates and blockages occur during purge,
    particularly after storms and cold weather.

-   No storm bypass hydraulics or insufficient headroom; upstream levels
    rise, screens misbehave and unseparated solids carry forward during
    peak events.

-   Detritor rake torque underrated; rakes stall or trip on first-flush
    loads, leaving grit to accumulate and undermining the intended
    free-vortex capture mechanism.

-   Omitting organics washing or setting loose limits results in wet,
    odorous grit with high putrescible content and materially higher
    disposal weights and fees.

subsections.title: KEY CONSTRUCTION CONSIDERATIONS
subsections.content:
-   Set channel and chamber levels to tight tolerances so velocities,
    depths and headloss match design calculations from minimum to storm
    conditions.

-   Install inlet guide arrangements/straighteners (where provided)
    accurately; support and seal to prevent vibration, ensuring
    consistent swirl development and robust long-term performance.

-   Cast and finish hopper and cone geometry with verified slopes,
    smooth transitions and true outlets so grit flows freely to
    extraction points without bridging.

-   For detritors, align centre column, rake mechanism and drive; prove
    scraper clearance, torque trip settings, travel stops and wear
    strips before wet commissioning.

-   Fit abrasion-resistant liners, hardened bends and sacrificial wear
    pieces at pump inlets and discharge elbows to protect primary
    pipework and prolong service life.

-   Provide trapped drainage, washdown water and containment around
    washers, conveyors and skips; segregate dirty drainage and manage
    aerosols to protect hygiene.

-   Commission lifting beams, removable covers and platforms early so
    nozzle maintenance, rake inspection and pump change-outs occur
    without unsafe improvisation.

-   Survey geometry, confirm self-cleansing velocities at low flows by
    water trials; demonstrate uniform swirl development and free
    discharge through launders and classifiers.

subsections.title: COMMON CONSTRUCTION ERRORS
subsections.content:
-   Tolerance drift at inverts or weirs alters velocities and detention
    time, undermining separation efficiency and forcing costly remedial
    works to recover performance.

-   Rough concrete, honeycombing or lips at joints catch grit, forming
    stubborn build-ups that resist flushing and require repeated manual
    cleaning interventions.

-   Unprotected cable routes cross access paths; bins and traffic damage
    services, causing unreliable purge, washing sequences and nuisance
    trips.

-   Poor sealing at pipe penetrations leaks abrasive slurry, stripping
    coatings and corroding reinforcement at structural interfaces around
    channels and chambers.

-   Detritor launders or classifier weirs not level, driving carryover,
    blockages and repeated strip-downs that erode commissioning time and
    maintenance resources.

subsections.title: KEY COMMISSIONING CONSIDERATIONS
subsections.content:
-   Prove separation efficiency with representative tests; log flows,
    swirl development parameters and captured mass versus the specified
    particle cut size to create baselines.

-   Optimise purge durations, pump speeds and cycle frequencies to clear
    hoppers completely while minimising water carryover, energy use and
    classifier loading.

-   Tune washer--classifier flows and vibration to meet dryness and
    organics limits; document repeatable results and sampling methods
    for operational acceptance.

-   Demonstrate failure and recovery: power dips, blocked suction, full
    skip alarms, rake stall/trip, safe isolation and restart procedures
    under witnessed conditions.

-   Verify instrumentation scaling, alarms and permissives integrate
    with SCADA; ensure trends and interlocks support timely maintenance
    and responsive operations.

-   Train operators on safe sampling, blockage clearance, nozzle
    maintenance, rake torque adjustment and purge set-up so day-to-day
    operation remains reliable.

-   Establish capture efficiency, moisture and organics baselines at
    low, average and storm flows; adjust inlet hydraulics and wash
    settings against clearly documented envelopes.

-   Set rake torque, slip-clutch and alarm thresholds; prove automatic
    purge sequences under load to prevent manual digging during first
    significant storms.

subsections.title: COMMON COMMISSIONING ERRORS
subsections.content:
-   Accepting steady-flow performance only; storm behaviour changes
    velocities and swirl patterns, reducing capture efficiency
    significantly under real operating conditions.

-   No baseline for grit moisture or organics; disposal costs and odour
    risk remain uncertain and disputes arise when receiving facilities
    reject or surcharge loads.

-   Over-conservative purge cycles waste water, overload classifiers and
    increase wear; too little purge allows hardening that becomes
    difficult and costly to recover.

-   Skipping blocked-line and pump-trip drills; responses are
    improvised, unsafe and slow during genuine upsets, prolonging
    downtime and increasing environmental risk.

-   Incomplete spares, tooling and maintenance schedules delay routine
    tasks; separation performance degrades long before expected service
    intervals are reached.

-   Hydraulic swirl not validated across the duty range; loss of swirl
    collapses separation, exporting grit downstream before operators can
    intervene effectively.

-   Detritor rake protections not proven; nuisance trips or stalled
    rakes leave accumulations that quickly overwhelm the system during
    first-flush events.

subsections.title: How it works
subsections.content:
Inlet pump stations lift raw sewage from the inlet works to the
hydraulic grade needed for downstream processes. A wet well (or separate
dry well) receives screened influent; pumps draw from correctly shaped
sumps to avoid vortices, air entrainment, and sediment build-up.
Arrangements commonly use submersible centrifugal pumps on guide rails
with auto-coupling pedestals, or dry-well pumps with flooded suction.
Control is by level instruments, start/stop bands, and alternation
logic; anti-ragging routines and backflush cycles reduce fouling. Duty,
duty/assist, and standby selections are set from design flow
distributions, storm risk, resilience targets, and allowable starts per
hour. Valved manifolds provide isolation, non-return protection, and
flow measurement interfaces. Safe access, lifting beams, washdown, and
hygienic drainage are essential, as are corrosion-resistant materials,
electrical segregation, and ventilation. Acceptance is proven by
matching pump curves to system head, stable control at turndown,
reliable debris management, and verified alarms and failover behaviour
aligned with standards and permit needs.

subsections.title: Key design considerations
subsections.content:
-   Define firm, duty, and standby capacity using realistic inflow
    distributions, resilience targets, allowable starts, and maintenance
    access envelopes.

-   Size sump plan area and depth to prevent vortices, air entrainment,
    and sediment deposition across minimum, average, and peak hydraulic
    operating conditions.

-   Provide approach hydraulics, inlet straight lengths, and stoplogs
    that deliver uniform velocities and safe isolation without starving
    any active pump intake.

-   Select impeller type, materials, and wear protection suitable for
    rags, grit loading, corrosive atmospheres, and the expected
    lifecycle maintenance strategy.

-   Allow N plus one electrical capacity, emergency power philosophy,
    and cable segregation compatible with resilience goals and
    foreseeable outage scenarios.

-   Integrate level, pressure, and flow instruments with control ranges,
    scaling, and cleaning access that suit fouling rates and provide
    stable process control.

-   Confirm minimum submergence and NPSH margins across temperature
    range; include anti-vortex measures validated by model guidance.

-   Review transients for starts, stops and power dips; specify surge
    mitigation, check-valve slam control and ramp profiles.

subsections.title: Common design errors
subsections.content:
-   Ignoring minimum submergence leads to air ingestion, vibration,
    cavitation, and repeated seal failures during turndown and variable
    seasonal temperature conditions.

-   Sump geometry forms dead zones where solids accumulate, harden, and
    require hazardous confined-space cleaning interventions at
    inconvenient operational times.

-   Insufficient inlet straight length creates skewed approach flow,
    unstable pump loading, and unreliable level control during
    alternation or duty assist transitions.

-   Duty selection overlooks ragging behaviour, causing blockages,
    reduced efficiency, and unscheduled manual de-ragging with
    significant safety and hygiene risks.

-   No positive isolation or bypass complicates maintenance, emergency
    operations, and overflow management during pump, valve, or
    instrumentation control failures.

-   Ignoring rag load leads to oversized inlets but clogged impellers;
    anti-ragging sequences and cutters not specified.

-   No overpumping connection or isolation per train, complicating
    maintenance and emergency bypass during commissioning or failures.

subsections.title: Key construction considerations
subsections.content:
-   Pour base slabs flat within tolerance, confirming sump depths and
    benching slopes achieve specified hydraulic performance under
    witnessed commissioning tests.

-   Install pumps square and true, aligning discharge spigots, guide
    rails, pedestals, and lifting chains without twist, interference, or
    docking misalignment issues.

-   Terminate cables with mechanical protection, drip loops, and
    segregation from lifting paths, preventing abrasion damage and
    accidental snagging by chains.

-   Seal wall penetrations, sleeves, and ducts to prevent leakage paths,
    corrosion staining, and long-term durability problems around
    structural and service interfaces.

-   Commission fixed lifting beams, sockets, and platforms with
    certificates, ensuring safe retrieval of pumps and screens during
    routine maintenance activities.

-   Provide washdown points, sump drains, and debris baskets with
    continuous falls, enabling hygienic housekeeping and preventing
    stagnation or odour generation locally.

-   Survey wet well levels and pump pedestal alignment; prove guide
    rails, docking cones and chain lengths before wet works.

-   Torque and label non-return and isolation valves; verify closing
    direction, stops and accessible lockable handles from platforms.

subsections.title: Common construction errors
subsections.content:
-   Guide rails misaligned or bent cause pump foot mismatch, excessive
    vibration, and failed docking under normal automatic start and stop
    cycling sequences.

-   Incorrect pedestal grouting leaves soft spots, misalignment, and
    premature wear on couplings, seals, and rotating elements during
    sustained operational duty.

-   Cable routes cross lifting zones, becoming damaged by chains, hooks,
    or bins, leading to shorts, trips, and avoidable reliability issues
    during operations.

-   Incomplete sealing around penetrations allows groundwater ingress,
    floatation risks, and persistent corrosion at reinforcement and
    embedded steel interfaces.

-   Missing access controls or inadequate edge protection expose
    personnel to falls, dropped objects, and unsafe improvisations while
    clearing rags or debris.

subsections.title: Key commissioning considerations
subsections.content:
-   Prove pump curves against system head by measuring duty points
    across speed steps, confirming predicted hydraulic performance and
    available operating turndown.

-   Validate start and stop levels, alternation logic, and anti-ragging
    routines using representative debris loads, recording adjustments
    and establishing operating baselines.

-   Demonstrate net positive suction head margins by observing noise,
    vibration, temperature, and motor parameters across seasonal and
    diurnal operating variations.

-   Establish debris management routines, flush cycles, and sump
    clean-down methods that maintain performance without exposing staff
    to unnecessary personal risk.

-   Verify instrument scaling, damping, and alarm thresholds deliver
    stable control, accurate trends, and timely notifications during
    transients and storm transitions.

-   Test emergency power sequences, black-start behaviour, and safe
    failover modes, ensuring containment and isolation during
    loss-of-utilities or control failures.

subsections.title: Common commissioning errors
subsections.content:
-   Accepting dry-run results as sufficient without proving hydraulic
    stability under real flows, temperatures, and debris loading at
    representative operating conditions.

-   Skipping reverse rotation checks on three-phase connections causes
    severe damage during first starts, later disputes, and unnecessary
    vendor warranty complications.

-   Leaving level instruments uncleaned or unshielded produces drifting
    readings, control hunting, and nuisance alarms that quickly erode
    operator confidence.

-   Not trending current draw, vibration, and discharge pressure hides
    incipient problems, leading to sudden failures without actionable
    early warning indicators.

-   Failing to rehearse pump outage, stuck valve, or bypass activation
    scenarios results in confusion, delays, and unsafe improvisation
    during genuine incident responses.

subsections.title: How it works
subsections.content:
Storm overflows protect upstream assets and treatment processes when
influent exceeds the works' treatment capacity. Under rising level, flow
is screened, split, and hydraulically controlled to divert excess to
storm storage and, only when permitted thresholds are exceeded, to the
environment through a consented overflow. Storage tanks attenuate peak
flows and capture first-flush pollutants; after the event, contents are
returned to inlet at controlled rates so primary and secondary processes
are not overwhelmed. Key functional elements include approach channels,
high-capacity screens, fixed or adjustable weirs, throttle structures,
detention volume, self-cleansing floor profiles, flushing systems, odour
control, and robust level/telemetry for event duration monitoring (EDM).
Design must reconcile headloss budget, spill frequency conditions, and
whole-life maintenance with safe access and cleaning. Structural
performance and watertightness are critical; joints, waterstops,
coatings, and ventilation must suit aggressive, intermittently wet
atmospheres. Commissioning proves hydraulics across scenarios, verifies
EDM and alarms, and demonstrates reliable emptying and cleaning so tanks
return rapidly to service without residual septicity or solids build-up.

subsections.title: Key design considerations
subsections.content:
-   Define consent conditions, allowable spill frequency, and EDM
    reporting requirements, then size storage volumes and throttles to
    achieve compliant outcomes across realistic storm distributions.

-   Fix approach hydraulics, weir crest levels, and screen sizing so
    headloss remains manageable, bypasses are controlled, and treatment
    flow is prioritised during dynamic rising situations.

-   Provide reliable return pumping and throttled rates that avoid
    shocking primary or biological stages, while ensuring timely
    emptying to recover resilience before subsequent storms.

-   Shape tank floors with self-cleansing slopes, effective flush
    systems, and drain-down points, reducing residual solids, septicity
    risk, and hazardous manual entry for cleaning operations.

-   Specify structural durability, joints, waterstops, and coatings for
    intermittent immersion, sulphide attack, and freeze-thaw, aligned
    with CESWI 8 durability and watertightness expectations.

-   Integrate odour containment, ventilation, safe access platforms,
    lifting provisions, and drainage segregation so routine inspection
    and maintenance remain practicable and defensible.

-   Provide fixed H2S detectors and alarm beacons at entries; interlock
    ventilation to protect confined space entries.

-   Specify EDM loggers with on-instrument buffering and tamper-evident
    clocks to preserve event evidence during outages.

subsections.title: Common design errors
subsections.content:
-   Storage volume calculated from unrealistic storm profiles, causing
    early spills, consent breaches, and reputational impacts when actual
    rainfall patterns diverge from assumed distributions.

-   Weir and throttle levels poorly coordinated with headworks
    hydraulics, producing uncontrolled backwater effects, upstream
    flooding, and degraded screening performance during peak conditions.

-   Screens undersized for debris and velocity, creating blinding,
    overtopping, rag carryover, and frequent maintenance interventions
    at precisely the wrong operational moments.

-   Return rates set without biological process input, overwhelming
    secondary treatment, driving solids washout, high turbidity, and
    unnecessary non-compliance risks after storm events.

-   Floors too flat with no organised flushing, leaving persistent
    deposits that harden, generate septicity, and demand hazardous,
    high-effort confined space cleaning activities.

-   Omitting dedicated vent capacity for rapid emptying creates
    vacuum/overpressure, damaging covers and drawing contaminants
    indoors.

-   No permanent jetting ports or CCTV access, making deposit removal
    reactive, costly and reliant on confined entries.

subsections.title: Key construction considerations
subsections.content:
-   Set weir crests, channel inverts, and throttle soffits to tight
    tolerances, verifying levels so the built hydraulic profile matches
    calculations through test fills and witnessed measurements.

-   Achieve watertight concrete construction with compliant joints,
    properly placed waterstops, compacted concrete, curing, and
    documented watertightness testing to agreed acceptance criteria.

-   Form floor falls and benching smoothly, free from steps and
    honeycombing, ensuring flush flows sweep deposits and that all
    drains reach accessible low points without dead legs.

-   Install large screens square, plumb, and well sealed to walls,
    preventing side bypass, vibration, and premature wear while enabling
    safe removal using designed lifting provisions.

-   Provide robust access, fall protection, lighting, and ventilation
    infrastructure early, so commissioning and maintenance activities
    can proceed without ad-hoc temporary arrangements.

-   Route cables, telemetry, and drainage so they are protected from
    traffic and flushing surges, maintaining reliability and avoiding
    damage during cleaning or storm operations.

-   Prove EDM chain: calibrate thresholds with staff gauges, photograph
    datum, and confirm SCADA timestamps match logger time.

-   Function-test throttle units under flow; verify orientation, stroke
    limits and fail-safe positions against drawings and cause--effect.

subsections.title: Common construction errors
subsections.content:
-   Crest levels drift during shuttering or finishing, shifting control
    points and undermining predicted spill behaviour despite apparently
    minor dimensional deviations at completion.

-   Poor joint detailing or mislocated waterstops create weeping paths,
    corrosion staining, and long-term durability problems that are
    expensive and disruptive to remediate later.

-   Rough, stepped floors trap solids, defeating flush systems and
    forcing intrusive manual clean-outs that could have been avoided
    with better finish quality assurance controls.

-   Screen frames twisted or inadequately grouted cause binding, leakage
    paths, and excessive vibration, shortening equipment life and
    increasing reactive maintenance burdens.

-   Inadequate ventilation duct routing and drainage segregation allow
    condensate and contaminated liquids to migrate into electrical
    areas, increasing corrosion and safety risks.

-   Ancillary storm equipment not on essential supplies; power dips
    disable screens or throttles, causing uncontrolled spills.

-   Level staff gauges, datum marks or benchmark bolts omitted, delaying
    acceptance and future recalibration of weir settings.

subsections.title: Key commissioning considerations
subsections.content:
-   Conduct controlled test fills to prove weir calibration, overflow
    initiation levels, throttle performance, and headloss, recording
    data against the design hydraulic acceptance envelope.

-   Verify EDM sensors, time synchronisation, data logging, and
    telemetry reporting, demonstrating event start and stop accuracy and
    reliable communication to regulatory systems.

-   Demonstrate full storage emptying at planned return rates without
    overwhelming downstream processes, confirming automatic sequences,
    permissives, and failure recovery behaviours.

-   Test flushing systems for coverage and effectiveness, confirming
    solids removal without dead zones and establishing routine cycle
    durations appropriate to seasonal operating conditions.

-   Prove odour control and ventilation performance, measuring airflow,
    negative pressure where required, and safe access conditions during
    and after storm tank emptying operations.

-   Complete operator training on storm scenarios, isolation steps,
    manual overrides, post-event inspections, and data review so
    responses are consistent, timely, and well documented.

-   Run staged multi-event simulations to prove empty-and-recover times
    before next storm; document capacity recovery curves.

-   Validate EDM end-to-end: trigger conditions, deadbands, rollover
    handling, and delayed-transfer buffering during communications or
    power loss.

subsections.title: Common commissioning errors
subsections.content:
-   Accepting dry commissioning only without wet hydraulic proofs,
    leaving weir calibration, throttle function, and overflow behaviour
    unverified under representative storm conditions.

-   Not validating EDM timestamps and thresholds, causing misreported
    events, regulatory challenges, and avoidable investigations when
    reported data conflicts with observed site conditions.

-   Skipping return-to-works rate optimisation leads to downstream
    process upsets, solids carryover, and repeated alarms during
    recovery periods following significant rainfall events.

-   Incomplete flushing coverage checks allow residual deposits that
    later harden, increasing odour, septicity risk, and manual cleaning
    requirements during operational periods.

-   Failure to rehearse failure modes and black-start sequences results
    in delays, confusion, and unsafe improvisation during genuine
    high-pressure storm incidents requiring rapid responses.

-   Accepting visual overflow onset without correlating EDM start level
    leads to misreported durations and regulatory challenges later.

-   Not proving safe oxygen levels and ventilation effectiveness before
    entry after storm returns; residual H2S pockets persist.

directSubsections.title: WHAT THIS STAGE INCLUDES
DESIGN BASELINES TO FIX BEFORE DETAILED DESIGN
directSubsections.content:
-   Defined design flows and peaking factors (dry, storm, first-flush).

-   Headloss budget across each unit and through bypasses/isolation
    points.

-   Screen type, bar spacing/open area, blinding rates, and duty/standby
    philosophy.

-   Grit plant selection (removal efficiency, vortex sizing, purge
    method).

-   Wet-well geometry, sump velocities, minimum submergence and
    anti-vortexing.

-   Storm control logic, storage sizing, return rates and protection of
    permit limits.

-   Access, lifting, washdown, drainage, odour containment and
    ventilation strategy.

-   Electrical segregation, resilience (power/controls), and data
    requirements.

-   Interfaces to MCERTS primary devices, samplers and sampling
    representativeness.

-   Define DSEAR zoning, ventilation rates, gas detection, and ATEX
    categories for enclosed channels and screenings areas.

-   Specify emergency bypass screening, headloss budget, positive
    isolation, and safe access for rehearsed under-flow operations.

-   Place MCERTS sampling points outside turbulence zones, with required
    straight lengths, isolation, and sampler access clearances.

-   Confirm washwater source, minimum pressure at winter temperatures,
    strainer specification, and appropriate backflow protection
    category.

-   Synchronise clocks across SCADA, EDM and MCERTS systems to ensure
    defensible timestamps for acceptance testing evidence.

-   Subject all changes to formal Management of Change, assessing
    permit, hydraulics, operability, programme, cost and risk.

directSubsections.title: CONSTRUCTION FOCUS
directSubsections.content:
-   Temporary works and excavation stability for deep inlets and wet
    wells.

-   Accurate levels for channels, stops, weirs and invert transitions.

-   Concrete durability, joints and waterstops to CESWI 8;
    watertightness testing.

-   Mechanical installation tolerances for screens and grit equipment.

-   Safe access routes, platforms and edge protection built into the
    permanent works.

-   Cable routing, earthing/bonding and segregation aligned with MEICA
    layouts.

-   Commissionability allowances: clean water supplies, washdown,
    drain-downs and bypass control.

-   Pressure or smoke-test odour covers and duct joints before wet
    testing; document leakage rates with calibrated instruments.

-   Install fixed platforms, ladders and rescue anchor points before wet
    works, enabling safe de-ragging and inspection access.

-   Prove penstocks, stoplogs and valves under flow; verify position
    indication, closing direction and accessible lockout points.

-   Verify washwater ring main pressure and flow at point-of-use;
    confirm strainers and backflow devices installed correctly.

-   Survey frames, hoppers, weirs and grout beds for level, plumb and
    square; rectify misalignment before equipment installation.

-   Complete earthing and bonding around metallic plant; install DSEAR
    zoning plates and safety signage before energisation.

directSubsections.title: COMMISSIONING FOCUS
directSubsections.content:
-   Dry and wet functional testing of each unit and bypass route.

-   Verification of headloss at representative flows and blinding
    conditions.

-   Screening capture rate, wash/compaction performance and export
    logistics.

-   Grit removal efficiency and purge/dewatering operation.

-   Pump control (start/stop levels, air-entrainment avoidance,
    auto-changeover).

-   Storm detection, storage fill/empty sequences, and controlled
    returns to headworks.

-   Alarms, trends and interlocks proven; data handed to ICA/SCADA with
    ranges and units.

-   Synchronise SCADA, telemetry and MCERTS clocks; verify audit trails
    produce aligned timestamps across all acceptance datasets.

-   Rehearse bypass introduction and reversion under controlled flows,
    confirming seals, debris containment, and operator procedures
    end-to-end.

-   Simulate loss of washwater, air and power; prove safe states,
    alarms, recoveries and automatic restarts function correctly.

-   Undertake end-to-end EDM tests with telemetry buffering, verifying
    definitive start and stop triggers for regulatory reporting.

directSubsections.title: RECORDS TO HAVE IN PLACE BEFORE MOVING ON
directSubsections.content:
-   Approved design basis (flows/loads), hydraulic profile and general
    arrangements.

-   ITPs with hold points for reinforcement inspection, pour release,
    watertightness/pressure tests, MEICA alignment checks and functional
    tests.

-   WIMES compliance evidence for packaged equipment; vendor FAT plans
    where applicable.

-   MCERTS design review for any inlet measurement or sampling
    interfaces.

-   Commissioning plan for staged proofs (dry, wet, storm scenarios) and
    acceptance criteria linked to the permit and downstream process
    needs.

-   Baseline headloss, capture and dryness curves for screens and
    washers, stored with instrument calibration references and
    timestamps.

-   Grit testing: mass captured versus flow, measured moisture and
    organics percentages, with washer-classifier configuration recorded.

-   Pumping: duty points versus system curve, vibration and current
    trends, reverse rotation checks, and alternation confirmations.

-   As-built surveys: levels, inverts, weirs and hoppers;
    watertightness, pressure tests, and coating inspection reports
    compiled.

-   EDM evidence pack: logic diagrams, telemetry tests, time-sync
    verification, and regulator-ready reports with event narratives.

title: Primary Treatment
content:
Primary treatment reduces the suspended solids and organic load entering
secondary processes by settling heavier particulate matter and removing
scum. Correctly designed and built primary units stabilise hydraulics,
reduce aeration energy demand, limit downstream fouling, and improve
final effluent quality by intercepting solids early in the process.
Success is measured by predictable hydraulic behaviour, reliable sludge
and scum removal, maintainable access, and clean interfaces to
biological treatment without shock loading.

subsections.title: How it works
subsections.content:
Primary settlement tanks remove settleable solids and floating scum
before biological treatment, lowering organic load and stabilising
downstream hydraulics. Influent is distributed to achieve calm, uniform
velocities and adequate residence time so heavier particles settle into
hoppers, while clarified flow passes over outlet weirs. Scum is directed
to a beach or dedicated removal mechanism and exported for treatment.
Sludge is withdrawn frequently to avoid septic conditions, excessive gas
production and rising blankets that can carry solids forward. Tank
geometry, inlet energy dissipation, baffle placement and outlet weir
configuration are coordinated to minimise short-circuiting and dead
zones. Structural detailing ensures watertightness and durability in
aggressive atmospheres with intermittent emptying. Mechanical elements
include scraper bridges or centre mechanisms, scum boards, draw-off
valves and sludge pumps sized for routine desludging without long
residence times. Performance is judged by stable effluent quality,
predictable sludge characteristics, reliable scum capture and
maintainable access. Civil works conform to CESWI 8, MEICA selections
follow WIMES expectations where applicable, and commissioning aligns
control, alarms and sampling to permit requirements and downstream
process needs.

subsections.title: Key design considerations
subsections.content:
-   Establish realistic flow distributions, diurnal patterns and storm
    interactions, then select surface overflow and solids loading rates
    that remain defensible throughout seasonal operating conditions.

-   Design inlet structures, energy dissipation and distribution baffles
    to promote uniform velocities, minimising short-circuiting and
    eddies that reduce effective residence time and separation
    efficiency.

-   Configure outlet launders and weirs for level uniformity, acceptable
    weir loading and access for cleaning, while preventing drawdown
    instabilities and unwanted air entrainment across the crest.

-   Size sludge hoppers with self-clearing slopes and draw-off pipework
    to minimise residence time, prevent septic conditions and reduce the
    risk of rising sludge during warm periods.

-   Provide reliable scum collection geometry, adjustable beach
    arrangements and discharge routes that avoid carryover, enable safe
    removal and support routine housekeeping expectations.

-   Specify structural durability, joints, waterstops and coatings
    suitable for intermittent immersion, hydrogen sulphide exposure and
    freeze--thaw effects expected at the specific project location.

-   Coordinate access, lifting, fall protection, lighting and washdown
    facilities so inspections, desludging and maintenance tasks are
    safely achievable without temporary improvisations or excessive
    downtime.

-   Check buoyancy and uplift stability for empty tanks at highest
    groundwater; design anti-float drainage and anchors.

-   Provide odour covers and ventilation duty to control H₂S exposure at
    launders and scum beaches in warm seasons.

subsections.title: Common design errors
subsections.content:
-   Assuming optimistic surface overflow rates that prove unsustainable
    during storms, leading to solids carryover, downstream upsets and
    increased aeration energy requirements in biological treatment
    stages.

-   Poor inlet energy dissipation causing jetting, uneven distribution
    and sludge re-suspension, which undermines settling performance and
    complicates commissioning adjustments under real operating
    conditions.

-   Weir levels not coordinated with hydraulics, creating preferential
    draw-off, localised scouring and persistent fouling that increases
    maintenance effort and reduces overall tank reliability
    significantly.

-   Hopper volumes oversized with long residence times, encouraging
    septic conditions, gas release and rising sludge that disrupts scum
    control and transfers solids to the next process unexpectedly.

-   Inadequate scum handling routes and containment, causing odour
    nuisance, unsafe manual interventions and frequent operational
    workarounds that elevate lifecycle costs unnecessarily.

-   Scraper drives undersized for peak solids and rag loads, leading to
    frequent stalls, vibration and prolonged outages.

-   Neglecting sludge draw-off routing with excessive bends causes
    blockages, requiring intrusive cleaning and commissioning delays.

subsections.title: Key construction considerations
subsections.content:
-   Cast bases, walls and baffles to tight level and line tolerances so
    hydraulic performance matches design expectations and distribution
    remains uniform across the tank plan area.

-   Form hopper slopes smooth and continuous, free from steps and
    honeycombing, ensuring settled solids flow reliably toward draw-off
    points without residual pockets requiring intrusive cleaning.

-   Install scraper mechanisms square, centred and correctly levelled,
    proving free rotation, correct clearances and alignment to hoppers,
    scum boards and drawoff orifices before wet testing.

-   Set weirs and launders true to level with uniform crest elevations,
    sealed joints and accessible cleaning provisions to avoid leakage
    paths, bypassing and chronic maintenance burdens.

-   Complete coatings, joint seals and waterstops per specification,
    then perform watertightness testing with documented acceptance
    criteria before introducing flow to the structure.

-   Provide permanent access systems, lifting points, lighting and
    washdown connections as designed, issuing certificates and ensuring
    safe use during commissioning and early operations stages.

-   Survey and record benchmark references around tank rim for future
    re-level checks and outlet weir adjustments.

-   Pre-install adjustable stoplogs or bulkhead guides to enable safe
    isolation and dewatering during future maintenance.

subsections.title: Common construction errors
subsections.content:
-   Differential settlement or poor finishing leaves baffles out of
    level, creating short-circuiting pathways that cannot be fully
    corrected by later commissioning adjustments or operational changes.

-   Honeycombing or rough surfaces in hoppers trap solids, resisting
    flow and requiring repeated confined-space cleaning, with increased
    risk and downtime for operational teams thereafter.

-   Misaligned scraper bridges or centre mechanisms cause rubbing,
    vibration and mechanical trips, shortening equipment life and
    delaying commissioning with unnecessary rectification activities.

-   Weir segments installed with inconsistent elevations lead to
    preferential draw-off, fouling and poor effluent uniformity,
    demanding rework or continuous operational compensations thereafter.

-   Incomplete sealing at penetrations and joints allows weeping,
    corrosion staining and durability issues that escalate into
    expensive remedial works during early operational life.

-   Scum baffles installed backwards or at incorrect height, causing
    persistent carryover and mandatory rework during commissioning.

-   Lifting eyes and davit sockets misplaced, obstructing scraper paths
    and necessitating intrusive modifications before safe removals.

subsections.title: Key commissioning considerations
subsections.content:
-   Prove hydraulic distribution with dye or tracer tests where
    practicable, then confirm settling performance using representative
    loads and agreed acceptance criteria for effluent clarity.

-   Establish desludging frequencies, valve positions and pump settings
    that maintain low residence times, stable blankets and reliable
    solids export across seasonal temperature variations.

-   Calibrate and verify level, flow and sludge blanket instruments,
    ensuring scaling, damping and alarm thresholds provide stable
    control and actionable operator indications consistently.

-   Optimise scum collection geometry, beach angles and removal routines
    so carryover is minimised, access remains safe and housekeeping
    demands stay within operational expectations sensibly.

-   Validate weir level uniformity and launder hydraulics under varying
    flows, documenting adjustments that achieve even draw-off and
    maintain compliant effluent transfer downstream.

-   Document start-up, shutdown, isolation and recovery sequences,
    including safe confined-space protocols, so operational responses
    are consistent during incidents and planned maintenance activities.

-   Trial torque-trip settings under increasing load to ensure
    protection without nuisance trips; record baseline acceptance
    values.

-   Test scraper restart after induced stall with debris present; verify
    auto-jog and safe manual clearing procedures.

subsections.title: Common commissioning errors
subsections.content:
-   Accepting performance at steady, moderate flows without testing
    storm conditions, missing behaviours that drive carryover, alarms
    and downstream process instability during rainfall events.

-   Failing to baseline sludge withdrawal rates and blanket depths,
    leaving operations without clear setpoints and allowing residence
    times to drift into septic, rising-sludge regimes inadvertently.

-   Leaving instruments uncalibrated or poorly located, causing noisy
    signals, nuisance alarms and operator distrust that undermines
    effective automatic control and timely interventions overall.

-   Neglecting scum system adjustments and cleaning routines, resulting
    in recurring carryover, odour issues and excessive manual handling
    risks for site personnel during routine operations.

-   Omitting clear recovery procedures for scraper trips, blocked
    drawoff or power interruptions, prolonging downtime and encouraging
    unsafe improvisation during genuinely stressful events.

-   Leaving scraper torque trips at default settings allows damage or
    nuisance trips, delaying stabilisation and inflating costs.

-   Failing to test manual scum removal routes causes unsafe
    improvisation during jams, prolonging outages and contaminating
    effluent.

subsections.title: How it works
subsections.content:
Primary sludge forms as settled solids in PST hoppers and must be
withdrawn frequently to prevent septicity, gas release and rising
sludge. Sludge is removed via draw-off lines to duty and standby pumps,
then transferred to thickening. Pipework requires adequate diameters,
short runs, minimal elbows, air release, drain points and reliable
isolation. Scum is collected at beaches or troughs and pumped or skimmed
to enclosed storage or dedicated handling, sometimes with heating to
prevent congealing. Controls coordinate desludge intervals, hopper
levels or blanket height targets with pump rates that protect hydraulics
and downstream treatment. Valving arrangements allow each tank to be
isolated, flushed and proven independently. Reliable operation depends
